Name: latent_variable_mixed_precision
Title: Dynamic Latent Variable Mixed-Precision Quantization for Adaptive Inference
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic adjustment of precision levels using a latent variable model can enhance the efficiency of mixed-precision quantization in deep learning models by aligning computation with data characteristics, thereby optimizing accuracy while minimizing latency.
Related Work: Existing methods in mixed-precision quantization generally utilize fixed or heuristic approaches for bit-width assignment, leading to suboptimal performance. Techniques such as EMQ and AMP-ViT offer advancements but lack a mechanism for real-time adaptation based on input data distributions. This proposal distinguishes itself by incorporating a latent variable model that learns to predict the optimal bit-width on-the-fly, allowing for more efficient adaptation during inference.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel framework for mixed-precision quantization that employs a latent variable model to dynamically adjust precision levels based on input data characteristics. By predicting optimal bit-width configurations in real-time, the framework can significantly improve the efficiency and accuracy of inference in neural networks. The proposed method will be validated through empirical studies, demonstrating its ability to maintain or enhance performance while reducing computational overhead. This approach not only addresses existing challenges in fixed or heuristic quantization methods but also leverages the strengths of adaptive learning, making it a promising avenue for future research in resource-constrained environments.
Experiments: 1. Develop a latent variable model that predicts optimal bit-widths based on statistical features of input data.
2. Implement a mixed-precision quantization framework that integrates the latent variable model into existing architectures (e.g., CNNs and Transformers).
3. Conduct experiments on standard datasets (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-10) to evaluate accuracy and latency trade-offs against state-of-the-art methods.
4. Use metrics such as throughput, accuracy drop, and latency overhead to assess performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The latent variable model may require extensive training data to generalize well, potentially limiting its effectiveness on unseen data.
Real-time adaptation may introduce additional overhead if not optimized, impacting the overall latency benefits.
The complexity of the model may lead to challenges in implementation and integration with existing architectures.
